jokes.text: Hello, I'd like to vote for president, governor, and anything that will take money away from our parks and libraries.
jokes.setup: Homer enters to vote, establishing civic responsibility
jokes.punchline: Casually admits wanting to defund public services
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - his selfish ignorance expressed as casual civic duty. Well-structured with the anti-climactic third item being the punchline.

jokes.text: Oh! I can't fit in the booth! Use the double-wide.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to enter normal voting booth
jokes.punchline: Poll worker matter-of-factly directs him to oversized booth
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy about Homer's size. Effective economy but not particularly original.

jokes.text: One vote for McCain. No, I want to vote for Obama. Two votes for McCain. Come on. It's time for a change! Three votes for McCain. No! No! No! Six votes for McCain.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to vote for Obama using electronic machine
jokes.punchline: Machine keeps registering McCain votes despite his protests, escalating absurdly
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure with the vote count jumping illogically from three to six. Timely political satire about voting machine concerns.

jokes.text: This doesn't happen in America! Maybe Ohio! But not in America!
jokes.setup: Homer outraged about rigged voting machines
jokes.punchline: Excludes Ohio from America due to voting controversies
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire with perfect comedic logic and economy. References real 2004 Ohio voting controversies with brilliant absurdist reasoning.

jokes.text: Montreal Expos jersey. Somber String. Jacks, no ball.
jokes.setup: Homer shopping for last-minute Christmas gifts
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific terrible gift options
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor with oddly specific bad gifts. 'Jacks, no ball' is particularly inspired as it renders the toy completely useless.

jokes.text: And a Slunky ... 'IT DOESN'T DO ANYTHING'
jokes.setup: Homer continues browsing terrible gifts
jokes.punchline: Product honestly advertises its complete uselessness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parody of Slinky with brutally honest marketing. The capitalized disclaimer makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, I wouldn't give these to my worst enemy, ... which happens to be Lisa.
jokes.setup: Homer evaluates the terrible quality of gifts
jokes.punchline: Reveals Lisa as his worst enemy, then decides they're perfect for her
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work revealing Homer's twisted parental logic. The pause before the revelation makes it land perfectly.

jokes.text: Girls don't like trucks! Come back when you're something else!
jokes.setup: Sentient toy truck approaches someone
jokes.punchline: Truck exhibits gender stereotypes and self-rejection
jokes.explanation: Creative absurdist concept of a self-aware, self-loathing gendered toy. Good setup for the Transformer parody.

jokes.text: Merry Christmas, Dad. We bought you three more minutes of oxygen.
jokes.setup: Bart giving Christmas gift to Homer
jokes.punchline: Gift is literally oxygen, measured in minutes
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about commodified air in the future, with the specificity of 'three minutes' making it absurdly precise.

jokes.text: I don't know how you could top last year's gift: a box of your burps.
jokes.setup: Lisa commenting on Bart's gift-giving history
jokes.punchline: Previous year's gift was literally packaged bodily functions
jokes.explanation: Good character-consistent humor showing Bart's creative awfulness. Sets up contrast for his surprisingly good gift.

jokes.text: Snooze! Threeway! Melody!
jokes.setup: Robots calling out their names as they transform
jokes.punchline: Names are inappropriately suggestive or mundane
jokes.explanation: Clever parody of Transformer naming conventions with adult humor mixed with mundane names like 'Melody.'

jokes.text: Wait a minute. We're missing Sex-Toy.
jokes.setup: Roll call of transformer names
jokes.punchline: Most inappropriate transformer name yet
jokes.explanation: Pushes the suggestive naming further but relies more on shock value than cleverness.

jokes.text: Where have you been? Where haven't I been?
jokes.setup: Question about Sex-Toy's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: Suggestive non-answer implying promiscuity
jokes.explanation: Predictable innuendo following the character name setup. More reliant on the name than the actual joke.

jokes.text: Well, the toaster's never lied to me before.
jokes.setup: Homer asking if kitchen looks different, toaster apparently responds 'NO'
jokes.punchline: Homer treats toaster as trusted advisor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - establishing a relationship history with an appliance. Great character consistency and absurdist reasoning.

jokes.text: Finally a boxing day that lives up to its name.
jokes.setup: Giant robots fighting outside during Christmas aftermath
jokes.punchline: Homer misunderstands Boxing Day as literal boxing
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay combining Homer's ignorance with the chaos outside. Good economy and timing.

jokes.text: It is a good planet to settle things on.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains robots chose Earth for their final battle
jokes.punchline: Robot casually agrees Earth is suitable for destruction
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery treating planetary destruction as a real estate decision. Understated but effective.

jokes.text: Stop it! Stop it! Look, you have made the nacho machine cry.
jokes.setup: Giant robots destroying convenience store
jokes.punchline: Homer focuses on anthropomorphized nacho machine's emotions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - amid apocalyptic destruction, he's concerned about a snack machine's feelings. Perfect character priorities.

jokes.text: That does not compute. Really?! It computes a little.
jokes.setup: Robot claims human logic doesn't compute
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiasm makes robot reconsider and partially compute it
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of robot dialogue tropes. The robot's honest reconsideration is unexpectedly human and funny.

jokes.text: You know I, I... I don't remember. Neither do I.
jokes.setup: Marge asks what their intergalactic war is about
jokes.punchline: Neither robot remembers why they're fighting
jokes.explanation: Good satirical point about meaningless conflicts. The robots' honesty and confusion work well.

jokes.text: And now that we are not fighting each other, we can team up to enslave your planet. That's great! Because working together, you can ... Oh ...
jokes.setup: Marge celebrates robots making peace
jokes.punchline: Peace means unified conquest of Earth; Marge realizes mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with Marge's dawning realization. The trailing off 'Oh...' sells the moment perfectly.

jokes.text: Okay, who's the idiot who taught them what foosball was? I thought they might enjoy it.
jokes.setup: Cut to robots apparently playing foosball with humans as the players
jokes.punchline: Lisa innocently introduced deadly game to killer robots
jokes.explanation: Dark visual comedy implied - humans being used as foosball players. Lisa's innocent intentions make it funnier.

jokes.text: Okay, Maggie, we'll be back in three hours. Or longer if something happens to us.
jokes.setup: Parents dropping Maggie at daycare
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of potential death as childcare contingency
jokes.explanation: Marge's matter-of-fact delivery of dark possibilities. Good parental anxiety humor with dark twist.

jokes.text: CRAZY ETHEL'S DAY CARE CENTER where your child learns to trust strangers
jokes.setup: Daycare center name and mission
jokes.punchline: Tagline promotes exactly what parents warn against
jokes.explanation: Sharp ironic contrast between parental fears and daycare marketing. Efficiently dark humor in signage.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, kids! Your old pal Krusty's gonna teach you five new words: Unlicensed use of my image.
jokes.setup: Krusty's cheerful greeting promising to teach kids words
jokes.punchline: The lesson is about intellectual property law
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection using children's TV format to deliver legal threats. Perfect Krusty characterization blending kid-friendly persona with mercenary business practices.

jokes.text: Entertain the troops? No way! What have they ever done for me?!
jokes.setup: Krusty asked to entertain military troops
jokes.punchline: Selfish refusal ignoring troops' service
jokes.explanation: Good Krusty characterization showing his complete self-centeredness. The irony of asking what troops have done for him lands well.

jokes.text: Well ... I'm no drunk driving.
jokes.setup: Ad executives praise Homer's talent for killing celebrities
jokes.punchline: Homer modestly compares himself to drunk driving as a celebrity killer
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Homer's twisted modesty. Treating drunk driving as the gold standard for celebrity deaths is brilliantly wrong.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, you finally did it. You mixed Buzz Cola with the smooth, rich taste of lemon.
jokes.setup: Dead Prince appears in commercial
jokes.punchline: Expresses amazement at basic cola-lemon combination as groundbreaking
jokes.explanation: Parodies celebrity endorsements by having the celebrity act amazed by mundane product features. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Sounds ... like ... quite ... a ... deal ... pilgrim! I hate ... trans ... action ... fees!
jokes.setup: Dead John Wayne forced to endorse financial services
jokes.punchline: Stilted delivery and modern concerns don't match his persona
jokes.explanation: Good parody of posthumous celebrity endorsements. The forced speech pattern and anachronistic concerns work well together.

jokes.text: That ad implies that Mr. Lincoln and I are... betraying familiarities!
jokes.setup: Washington objects to wedding chapel ad featuring both presidents
jokes.punchline: Uses euphemistic language for romantic implications
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate euphemistic outrage. Washington's formal language contrasting with the situation is effective.

jokes.text: Put a sock in it, mushmouth!
jokes.setup: Lincoln giving diplomatic speech about taking law into own hands
jokes.punchline: Wayne cuts him off with crude dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good character clash between Lincoln's diplomacy and Wayne's bluntness. 'Mushmouth' is a perfect Wayne-style insult.

jokes.text: Great party, Homer! Murdering Kate Winslet paid for that chocolate fountain.
jokes.setup: Homer hosting party in heaven
jokes.punchline: Casually credits specific murder for party funding
jokes.explanation: Dark humor treating murder as a business transaction. Homer's casual delivery makes the darkness funnier.

jokes.text: Ah, you ripped me off. See? No, I didn't. See! You're gonna pay for what you done. See!
jokes.setup: Dead gangster confronting Homer
jokes.punchline: Classic gangster speech pattern with 'See!'
jokes.explanation: Standard gangster parody. Functional character voice but predictable material.

jokes.text: I always thought I'd die of hepatitis ... C.
jokes.setup: Krusty being attacked by dead celebrities
jokes.punchline: Expected different cause of death based on lifestyle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty moment acknowledging his dissolute lifestyle. The specific strain of hepatitis shows attention to character detail.

jokes.text: Rip Taylor? You're not even dead! Someone needs to check my apartment.
jokes.setup: Homer confronted by supposedly dead celebrity
jokes.punchline: Celebrity reveals he might actually be dead and undiscovered
jokes.explanation: Dark twist on celebrity death jokes. The implication that he's been dead alone in his apartment is genuinely unsettling and funny.

jokes.text: Well, your wife likes it.
jokes.setup: Ernest Hemingway asks if Homer's mustache is coming or going
jokes.punchline: Homer uses classic 'your wife' comeback
jokes.explanation: Generic comeback joke. Gets points for Homer's confidence but lacks originality or cleverness.

jokes.text: It's a mix of voodoo and Methodist.
jokes.setup: Homer asks what the one true religion is before dying
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific and contradictory religious combination
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd religious combination. The specific pairing of voodoo and Methodist creates perfect cognitive dissonance.

jokes.text: What did you say, Mom? Nothing. I'm practicing my trombone.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks about Marge's witch comment
jokes.punchline: Marge pretends trombone makes witch-like sounds
jokes.explanation: Clever excuse for making witch sounds. The visual of Marge awkwardly playing trombone to cover adds to the humor.

jokes.text: I believe in the Grand Pumpkin, almighty gourd, who was crustified over Pontius Pie-plate, and ascended into oven. He will come again to judge the filling and the bread ...
jokes.setup: Milhouse reciting his pumpkin faith
jokes.punchline: Apostles' Creed rewritten with baking terminology
jokes.explanation: Masterful religious parody with consistent baking terminology. 'Crustified' and 'Pontius Pie-plate' are inspired wordplay. Demonstrates sophisticated writing and commitment to the bit.

jokes.text: You are such a stupid moron! It makes people want to punch you! The Grand Pumpkin's superga-ay! Punch, punch, punch, punch, wedgie!
jokes.setup: Nelson responding to pumpkin carols
jokes.punchline: Narrates his bullying actions while performing them
jokes.explanation: Classic Nelson behavior with the added humor of verbalizing his physical actions. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: More Granny Smiths for me.
jokes.setup: Others leaving apple bobbing for pumpkin patch
jokes.punchline: Bart excited about less competition for specific apple variety
jokes.explanation: Bart's opportunistic nature and specific apple knowledge. Simple but character-appropriate humor.

jokes.text: You owe me restitution!
jokes.setup: Milhouse practicing what he'll say to Grand Pumpkin
jokes.punchline: Uses legal term inappropriately in religious context
jokes.explanation: Good Milhouse moment mixing childish excitement with sophisticated vocabulary inappropriately.

jokes.text: Oh, how delightful-- bread made especially for pumpkins. Actually, it's made from pumpkins.
jokes.setup: Grand Pumpkin excited about pumpkin bread gift
jokes.punchline: Discovers bread contains his own kind
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection about the bread's purpose versus ingredient. The Grand Pumpkin's horror at cannibalism is well-executed.

jokes.text: I'm gonna give you crossed eyes like you might see on an idiot, a stupid triangle nose and a big mouth full of the ugliest shaped teeth there are ... square.
jokes.setup: Grand Pumpkin threatening to carve Milhouse
jokes.punchline: Describes jack-o'-lantern features as specific punishments, ending with 'square' teeth
jokes.explanation: Creative threat that turns pumpkin carving into specific insults. The escalation to square teeth as the ultimate ugliness works well.

jokes.text: Oh, God! Everywhere I look-- pumpkin atrocities!! Care for a pumpkin seed? You roast the unborn?
jokes.setup: Grand Pumpkin sees Halloween party
jokes.punchline: Views normal activities as genocide
jokes.explanation: Brilliant perspective flip - innocent snacks become horrific from pumpkin viewpoint. 'Roast the unborn' is inspired.

jokes.text: You roast the unborn?
jokes.setup: Someone offers Grand Pumpkin pumpkin seeds
jokes.punchline: Pumpkin horrified at eating roasted seeds (pumpkin babies)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reframing of pumpkin seeds as unborn pumpkins. The horror in the phrasing makes common Halloween snacks sound monstrous.

jokes.text: You're a racist! All pumpkins are racist. The difference is I admit it!
jokes.setup: Grand Pumpkin dismisses yellow pumpkin as lesser
jokes.punchline: Admits all pumpkins are racist but claims honesty as virtue
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about systematic prejudice with the pumpkin claiming honesty makes his racism better. Unexpected character depth.

jokes.text: No. Who is he and what's his origin story?
jokes.setup: Lisa asks if Milhouse has heard of Tom Turkey
jokes.punchline: Milhouse asks for complete backstory like a comic book character
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Milhouse's comic book mindset applied to mythology. Meta-humor about narrative construction.

jokes.text: Um ... well, Tom is a magical turkey who gave the pilgrims the technology to put buckles on their hats.
jokes.setup: Lisa improvising Tom Turkey mythology
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous historical claim about hat buckle technology
jokes.explanation: Absurdist historical revision making hat buckles a magical technology. The specificity and silliness work well together.

jokes.text: A giant talking turkey? Preposterous!
jokes.setup: Tom Turkey appears to fight Grand Pumpkin
jokes.punchline: Giant talking pumpkin calls turkey preposterous
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - supernatural being dismissing another as impossible. Great self-awareness and timing.

jokes.text: You eat turkeys?! Oh, yeah. It's delicious! Especially when we take stuffing and shove it up the turkey's ... Revenge!!!!
jokes.setup: Tom Turkey asks about Thanksgiving feast
jokes.punchline: Homer enthusiastically describes turkey preparation before Tom realizes the horror
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation with Homer's oblivious enthusiasm about turkey preparation leading to Tom's horror. Great timing and character work.

jokes.text: Looks like those kids found the true spirit of Halloween. Or Thanksgiving. Or whatever.
jokes.setup: Chaos with multiple holiday monsters
jokes.punchline: Confused about which holiday applies
jokes.explanation: Good meta commentary on the segment's holiday confusion. Functions as wrap-up but not deeply funny.

jokes.text: And for those of you who feel like we've trampled on a beloved children's classic, I encourage you to write to the following address.
jokes.setup: Marge addressing audience concerns about parodying Peanuts
jokes.punchline: Dismissively directs complaints to an address (presumably useless)
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor acknowledging the parody while dismissing criticism. Good fourth-wall breaking moment typical of Simpsons endings.
